summary:
Market Analysis: Government of Canada (GoC) Yield Volatility and Margin Outlook

The GoC bond market has experienced a significant "bear flattener" over the past 30 days, with yields surging in March following a mid-February lull. Since late February, the 2-year yield has climbed approximately 56 basis points to 2.95%, while the 5-year yield has risen 47 basis points to 3.14%. This upward repricing is driven by a hawkish shift in market sentiment following last week's FOMC meeting and recent Canadian CPI data, which suggests that inflation remains "sticky" above the 2% target. As of March 25, 2026, the narrowing of the 2Y/5Y spread from 36bps to 19bps indicates that markets are aggressively pricing in a "higher-for-longer" policy stance from the Bank of Canada, heavily influenced by similar upward pressure in U.S. Treasuries as the Fed delays its easing cycle.

For our Credit Union, this volatility necessitates an immediate tactical review of our retail pricing. The sharp rise in the 5-year yield—the primary benchmark for 5-year fixed-rate mortgages—will require an upward adjustment to our mortgage prime to maintain institutional spreads. Simultaneously, the surge in the 2-year yield will increase the cost of term deposits; as GIC rates track the 2-year curve, we expect intensified competition for liquidity, which may compress our Net Interest Margin (NIM). Given the high correlation between GoC yields and U.S. Treasuries, we remain vulnerable to "imported" volatility from south of the border. Management should prepare for a period of tighter margins as the cost of funds rises faster than our ability to reprice the existing loan book, necessitating a defensive posture in our liquidity management for the remainder of Q1.
